Idiomatic Expressions

  • 經亂衰翁居破村 - Classical structure describing the aftermath of turmoil: 'After the chaos, an old man lives in a ruined village.'
  • 村中何事不傷防 - Rhetorical question emphasizing the extent of the devastation: 'In the village, what is not damaged or destroyed?'
  • 因供寨木無桑柘 - Resource depletion due to military needs: 'Because he supplies wood for the fortifications, there are no mulberry or elm trees.'
  • 為着鄉兵絶子孫 - Severe consequences of military service on family continuity: 'For the sake of serving as a local militia, his descendants are cut off.'
  • 至於雞犬皆星散 - Vivid description of chaos and disarray: 'Even chickens and dogs scatter like stars.'
  • 日落前山獨倚門 - Poignant image of solitude and desolation: 'At sunset, he stands alone by the door of the front mountain.'
  • 夫因兵死守蓬茅 - Widow's plight: 'The husband died in battle, leaving her to guard the thatched hut.'
  • 桑柘廢來猶納稅 - Unjust burden on the poor: 'Even though the mulberry and elm trees have been abandoned, she still pays taxes.'
  • 時挑野菜和根煮 - Extreme poverty and struggle for survival: 'She sometimes picks wild vegetables and cooks them with their roots.'
  • 旋斫生柴帶葉燒 - Rudimentary and difficult process of gathering firewood: 'She chops fresh firewood and burns it with leaves.'
  • 任爾深山更深處 - Hardships are everywhere: 'No matter how deep into the mountains you go.'
  • 也應無計避征徭 - Inevitability of suffering under the oppressive system: 'There should be no way to avoid the corvée and taxes.'
  • 握手相看誰敢言 - Fear and silence among the people: 'Holding hands and looking at each other, who dares to speak?'
  • 軍家刀劍在要邊 - Constant threat of violence: 'The military's swords and spears are at the critical border.'
  • 亂殺平人不怕天 - Brutality and lack of moral restraint: 'They kill ordinary people without fearing the heavens.'
  • 古寺拆為修寨木 - Destruction of cultural heritage for military purposes: 'The ancient temple is dismantled to build fortifications.'
  • 荒墳掘作甃城磚 - Desecration and exploitation: 'The abandoned graves are dug up to make bricks for the city walls.'
  • 郡侯逐去渾閒事 - Insignificance of local governance in the face of larger chaos: 'The prefect is driven away, and it is all trivial.'
  • 正是鑾輿幸蜀年 - Specific historical event, the An Lushan Rebellion: 'It is indeed the year when the imperial carriage travels to Shu.'
  • 然方之今日始信其非寓言也 - Described events are not just fictional but real and ongoing: 'But when compared to today, it is only then that one believes it is not a fable.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 于天子象曰 - Phrase meaning 'The image of the Son of Heaven says,' where '天子' refers to the emperor and '象曰' is a classical formula meaning 'the image says.'
  • 同居之义 - Refers to the principle of cohabitation or living together harmoniously, possibly in a political or social context.
  • 忍克致也 - Phrase that can be interpreted as 'enduring and overcoming leads to success' or 'perseverance brings achievement.'
  • 上六血气方刚戒之在鬬 - Phrase that can be translated as 'At the uppermost level, when one's blood and qi are strong, one must be cautious of conflict' or 'When one is at the peak of physical strength, one should be wary of fighting.'
  • 方刚之气忍则灭也 - Means 'The strong and vigorous qi, if endured, will be extinguished' or 'The fierce energy, if suppressed, will be destroyed.'
  • 形而为鬬自求祸也 - Means 'If it takes physical form as a fight, it will invite disaster' or 'If it manifests as conflict, it will bring its own misfortune.'
  • 其黙卦曰黙无咎可贞不利有所言 - Phrase that can be interpreted as 'The hexagram of silence says: Silence brings no blame, it is favorable to be steadfast, and it is not advantageous to speak.'
  • 黙不言也乱之所生也 - Means 'Silence, not speaking, is where chaos arises' or 'Silence, not uttering words, is the source of disorder.'
  • 言语以为阶 - Means 'Words serve as a ladder' or 'Speech is the means by which one ascends.'
  • 黙以自守其道可贞 - Means 'Silence protects one's way, it is favorable to be steadfast' or 'Silence preserves one's path, it is good to remain firm.'
  • 不利有所言 - Means 'It is not advantageous to speak' or 'It is not beneficial to say anything.'
  • 尚口乃穷也 - Means 'To value speech is to be in poverty' or 'To rely on words is to be in a state of destitution.'
  • 守口如瓶 - Common idiom meaning 'to keep one's mouth shut like a bottle' or 'to be silent as a sealed jar.'
  • 多言不如守中 - Means 'Much speaking is not as good as maintaining the middle way' or 'Excessive talk is not as beneficial as staying centered.'

  • 上六 - Term from the *I Ching*, referring to the sixth line of the upper trigram, which is often associated with the highest or most extreme position.
  • 黙卦 - Refers to a hexagram in the *I Ching* that emphasizes the virtue of silence.
  • 圣人 - Refers to a sage or wise person, often associated with Confucian ideals of wisdom and virtue.

  • 其后绛州公库乃得其一扵是补刻余帖名东库本 - Later, the public treasury of Jizhou obtained one part and supplemented the rest, naming it the *Dongku Version*.
  • 第九卷之舛误盖始于此 - The errors in the ninth volume began here.
  • 且逐卷逐叚各分字号以日月光天徳等二十字为次第 - Each volume and section is numbered using the characters 日 (sun), 月 (moon), 光 (light), 天 (heaven), 德 (virtue), etc., in a specific order.
  • 后避金主亮讳但庾亮帖内亮字皆去右边转笔谓之亮字不全本 - Later, to avoid offending the name of Jin Emperor Liang, all 'liang' characters in Yu Liang's calligraphy were modified by removing the right stroke, known as the 'incomplete liang' version.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 新绛本北方别本 - Refers to a northern variant of the Xinning edition of the 'Xinning Stone Rubbings'.
  • 武冈新旧本 - Refers to both new and old versions of the 'Wugang Stone Rubbings'.
  • 福清乌镇彭州资州本木本 - Refers to various editions of the 'Fuzhou Stone Rubbings', 'Wuzhen Stone Rubbings', 'Pengzhou Stone Rubbings', and 'Zizhou Stone Rubbings', with '木本' indicating wooden block prints.
  • 前十巻等皆绛帖之别也 - Indicates that the first ten volumes and others are all variants of the 'Xinning Stone Rubbings'.
  • 潭帖者庆歴中刘丞相帅潭日以淳化官帖命慧照大师希白模刻于石置之郡斋 - Refers to the 'Tantie' (潭帖), a collection of calligraphy models compiled during the Qingli period by Liu Chengxiang, the Chancellor, who commissioned Master Huizhao Xi Bai to carve the 'Chunhua Official Rubbings' onto stone and place it in the prefectural hall.
  • 增入伤寒十七日王蒙顔真卿诸帖而字行颇髙与淳化阁本差不同 - Indicates that additional works such as 'Shanghan Shiqi Ri' (伤寒十七日) and 'Wang Meng' (王蒙) were added, and the font size is larger than that of the 'Chunhua Official Rubbings'.
  • 逐卷有慧照大师希白重模字而嵗月各异中间缪处甚多 - Each volume has the inscription 'Master Huizhao Xi Bai re-carved' with different dates, and there are many errors.
  • 朱文公讥其极为可笑者是也 - Refers to Zhu Xi (朱熹) criticizing the 'Tantie' as extremely laughable.
  • 潭帖之别则有刘丞相私第本长沙碑匠新刻本三山木本蜀本庐陵萧氏本等甚多 - Lists various editions of the 'Tantie', including private editions of Liu Chengxiang, new carvings by stonemasons in Changsha, wooden block prints from San Mountain, Sichuan editions, and editions from Luxian in Lu陵.
  • 戯鱼即临江帖也 - Refers to the 'Xiyu Tie' (戏鱼帖), which is another name for the 'Linjiang Tie' (临江帖).
  • 元祐间刘次荘以家蔵淳化阁帖十卷摹刻于戱鱼堂除去题而増文 - During the Yuanyou period, Liu Cizhuang copied the ten volumes of the 'Chunhua Official Rubbings' in his house and carved them in the 'Xiyu Hall', removing the colophons and adding new texts.
  • 庆元中四川总领权安节又重摹扵利州黔江者黔人秦世 - During the Qingyuan period, Quan Anjie, the military governor of Sichuan, had the 'Xiyu Tie' recarved in Qianjiang, Lizhou, by the Qian person Qin Shi.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 丰比诸刻为肥 - A phrase comparing the quality or appearance of a particular version of a text, possibly the 'Lan亭序,' with other versions. '丰' means 'plump' or 'full,' and '肥' means 'fat' or 'rich.' The phrase suggests that this version is richer or fuller in some way.
  • 潭绛则太痩 - '潭绛' likely refers to a specific version or copy of the 'Lan亭序,' and '太痩' means 'too thin' or 'too lean.' This suggests that this version is lacking in richness or fullness.
  • 临江则太媚 - '临江' likely refers to another version or copy of the 'Lan亭序,' and '太媚' means 'too charming' or 'too pretty.' This suggests that this version is overly ornate or lacking in substance.
  • 用李廷珪墨印造 - '李廷珪' was a famous ink maker during the Song Dynasty. '用...墨印造' means 'using...ink to print,' indicating that the text was printed using ink made by Li Tinggui.
  • 余始得汪端明所记阁帖行数 - '余' means 'I,' '始得' means 'first obtained,' '汪端明' is a person's name, '所记' means 'recorded,' and '阁帖行数' refers to the number of lines in a collection of calligraphy. This phrase indicates that the author first obtained a record of the number of lines in a collection of calligraphy from Wang Duanming.
  • 晚使江左用二千楮致一本 - '晚' means 'later,' '使' means 'sent,' '江左' refers to the area south of the Yangtze River, '用' means 'with,' '二千楮' means 'two thousand sheets of paper,' and '致' means 'to obtain.' This phrase indicates that later, the author sent two thousand sheets of paper to the south of the Yangtze River to obtain a copy of the text.
  • 尤伯晦见之曰寳物也 - '尤伯晦' is a person's name, '见之' means 'saw it,' and '寳物也' means 'a treasure.' This phrase indicates that You Bohui saw the text and called it a treasure.
  • 真帖可辨者有数条 - '真帖' means 'authentic text,' '可辨者' means 'that can be distinguished,' and '有数条' means 'there are several.' This phrase indicates that there are several ways to distinguish an authentic text.
  • 墨色一也 - '墨色' means 'ink color,' and '一也' means 'one thing.' This phrase indicates that the ink color is one distinguishing feature.
  • 他本刋巻数在上版数在下 - '他本' means 'other versions,' '刋巻数' means 'number of volumes,' and '版数' means 'number of pages.' This phrase indicates that in other versions, the number of volumes is listed above the number of pages.
  • 惟此本巻数版数字皆相聨属 - '惟此本' means 'only this version,' '巻数' means 'number of volumes,' '版数' means 'number of pages,' and '相聨属' means 'are connected.' This phrase indicates that in this version, the numbers of volumes and pages are connected.
  • 他本行数字比帖字小而瘦 - '他本' means 'other versions,' '行数字' means 'number of lines,' '帖字' means 'characters in the text,' and '小而瘦' means 'small and thin.' This phrase indicates that in other versions, the number of lines is small and the characters are thin.
  • 此本行数字比帖中字皆大而浓 - '此本' means 'this version,' '行数字' means 'number of lines,' '帖中字' means 'characters in the text,' and '大而浓' means 'large and thick.' This phrase indicates that in this version, the number of lines is large and the characters are thick.
  • 余所得江左本每版皆全纸无接黏处 - '余所得' means 'what I obtained,' '江左本' means 'the version from the south of the Yangtze River,' '每版' means 'each page,' '皆全纸' means 'all whole sheets,' and '无接黏处' means 'no splices.' This phrase indicates that each page of the version from the south of the Yangtze River is a whole sheet with no splices.
  • 一部十卷无一版不与端明所记合 - '一部' means 'a set,' '十卷' means 'ten volumes,' '无一版' means 'no page,' '不与' means 'does not match,' and '端明所记' means 'what Wang Duanming recorded.' This phrase indicates that every page of the set of ten volumes matches what Wang Duanming recorded.
  • 欲旧帖之真面目 - '欲' means 'to preserve,' '旧帖' means 'old text,' '之' means 'of,' and '真面目' means 'true appearance.' This phrase indicates that the goal is to preserve the true appearance of the old text.

  • 君幸毋泄吾恐院使不久生矣 - Means 'please do not reveal this, I fear the Vice-Inspector will not live long.' Expresses a request for secrecy and a concern about the Vice-Inspector's health.
  • 众问其故 - Means 'the others asked the reason.' Indicates a query about the cause of something.

  • 有报院使已暴卒 - Means 'there was a report that the Vice-Inspector had suddenly died.' Describes a sudden and unexpected death.

  • 虽取所喜者为之亦不能如意也 - Means 'even if I try to do what I like, I cannot achieve satisfaction.' Expresses frustration and dissatisfaction.

  • 喜承日用轻安 - Means 'I am pleased to hear that you are living a peaceful life.' Expresses a positive sentiment.
  • 所湏诸方既无人可抄又意绪不佳嬾动耳 - Means 'the various places where I need to go have no one to accompany me, and my mood is poor, so I am lazy to move.' Describes a lack of motivation and companionship.

  • 用贯众一斤细剉如骰子 - Means 'use one catty of *Gancao* (a type of herb), finely chopped like dice.' Describes the preparation of the herb.

  • 翻覆令展尽余汁 - Means 'turn them over to spread out all the remaining juice.' Describes a step in the drying process.
  • 簸取黑豆去贯众空心日防五七粒食 - Means 'winnow to separate the black beans from the *Gancao*, eat five or seven grains on an empty stomach each day.' Describes the consumption of the beans.
  • 百草木枝叶皆有味可饱也 - Means 'all branches and leaves of hundred kinds of grass and trees have flavor and can be eaten to satisfy hunger.' Suggests that many plants can be used as food.

  • 紫緑万状变幻顷刻恍可人目 - Changing colors of the sky or landscape, shifting rapidly and visually stunning; '紫緑' refers to purple and green colors, '万状' means countless forms or variations, '变幻' means to change or transform, and '恍可人目' means to be dazzling or captivating to the eyes.
  • 牛背笛声两两来归而月印前溪矣 - Sound of flutes coming from the backs of cattle as they return home, with the moon reflected in the stream ahead; '牛背笛声' refers to the sound of flutes played on the backs of cattle, and '月印前溪' means the moon is reflected in the stream ahead.
  • 味子西此句可谓絶妙 - The flavor of the sentence from the West is truly exquisite; '味子西' refers to the essence or flavor of something from the West, and '絶妙' means absolutely wonderful or exquisite.
  • 然此句妙矣识其妙者盖少 - Although this sentence is wonderful, those who recognize its beauty are few; '识其妙者' refers to those who understand or appreciate the beauty, and '盖少' means 'are few'.
  • 彼牵黄臂苍驰猎扵声利之塲者 - Those who are driven by the pursuit of fame and profit, often engaging in hunting or other competitive activities; '牵黄臂苍' refers to pulling a yellow dog and wearing a dark coat, which is a metaphor for someone who is actively pursuing something, and '声利之塲' means the field of fame and profit.
  • 但见衮衮马头尘匆匆驹隙影耳 - Fleeting nature of life, with people rushing by like dust on the road and like shadows passing quickly; '衮衮马头尘' refers to the dust kicked up by horses, and '匆匆驹隙影' means the fleeting shadow of a horse passing by.
  • 乌知此句之妙哉 - How could they know the beauty of this sentence? '乌知' is a rhetorical question meaning 'how could they possibly know,' and '此句之妙' refers to the beauty of the sentence.
  • 人能真知此妙则东坡所谓无事此静坐一日是两日若活七十年便是百四十所得不已多乎 - If a person truly understands this beauty, then as Su Shi (Su Dongpo) said, 'If there is nothing to do, sitting quietly for one day is like two days, and living seventy years is like living one hundred and forty years. Isn't that a great gain?' '东坡' refers to Su Shi, a famous poet and scholar, and '无事此静坐一日是两日' means 'if there is nothing to do, sitting quietly for one day is like two days.'
  • 此罗君语也 - This is what Luo Jun said; '罗君' refers to Luo Jun, a person mentioned in the text.
  • 风尘洞豺虎咬人防赋归与之诗 - Dangers and hardships of life, with '风尘洞' referring to the chaos and turmoil of the world, and '豺虎咬人' meaning 'being bitten by wolves and tigers,' which is a metaphor for facing danger or adversity; '防赋归与之诗' means 'to compose a poem about returning home.'
  • 计无所得又未知何日可以遂吾志也 - I have nothing to gain, and I do not know when I will be able to achieve my aspirations; '计无所得' means 'I have nothing to gain,' and '遂吾志' means 'achieve my aspirations.'
  • 掩卷为之三叹日书三万字 - Closing the book, I sighed three times and wrote thirty thousand words; '掩卷' means 'closing the book,' '为之三叹' means 'sighed three times,' and '书三万字' means 'wrote thirty thousand words.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 江浙平章子山公 - High-ranking official in the Jiang-Zhe region (modern-day Jiangsu and Zhejiang provinces) during the Yuan Dynasty; '平章' is a title for a high-ranking official, and '子山公' is the name of the person.

  • 康里人 - Person from the region of Kangli, which is now part of modern-day Xinjiang; '康里' is a place name, and '人' means 'person.'

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 草玉露一两叚再烹苦茗一杯出歩溪边邂逅园翁溪友 - This long sentence describes a series of actions and encounters. The phrase can be broken down into smaller parts for clarity: '草玉露一两叚' (after a moment of reflection), '再烹苦茗一杯' (then brewed a cup of bitter tea), '出歩溪边' (went out to the stream), '邂逅园翁溪友' (encountered a wise old man and a friend).
  • 问桑麻说秔稲量晴校雨探节数时 - This phrase is a list of activities related to agriculture and weather. Each action is connected by the conjunction '说' (to talk about), making it a series of related but distinct actions.
  • 归而倚杖柴门之下则夕阳在山紫緑万状变幻顷刻恍可人目 - This phrase describes a scene of returning home and observing the changing colors of the sky. The sentence structure is complex.

  • 书吏 - A clerk or scribe who works in official government offices.
  • 夜坐私第一室 - Phrase meaning 'sat alone in the first private room at night,' suggesting solitude or secrecy.
  • 钧防呼唤 - Formal term for a summons or call from a superior, likely from the head office or '都堂' (the main administrative hall).
  • 上马頴前导 - 'Rode up with a guide in front,' suggesting a hurried or urgent journey.
  • 大官危坐听事 - 'A high official sat upright listening to the matter,' indicating a formal audience or judgment.
  • 河南饥省咨至 - 'A report from the Henan province about a famine reached the office,' with '饥' meaning famine, '省咨' a report from a province.
  • 缓七日不报 - 'Delayed for seven days without reporting,' indicating negligence or failure to respond.
  • 死者甚众 - 'Many people died,' a common expression for large-scale loss of life.
  • 某提控耳 - 'I am only a controller (提控),' indicating the speaker's modest rank or position.
  • 该掾稽迟之罪 - 'The fault of the clerk for the delay,' referring to the official responsible for the delay.
  • 呈举官 - 'Submitted the case to the official for judgment,' indicating the process of presenting a matter for review.
  • 非汝过也 - 'It is not your fault,' a formal way of absolving someone.
  • 汝退 - 'You may go back,' a command to the speaker.
  • 急追该掾某人来 - 'Urgently summon that clerk, someone, to come,' indicating a call for immediate action.
  • 梦觉也 - 'I awoke from the dream,' indicating the end of the vision or dream.
  • 令人觇之夜 - 'Sent someone to spy at night,' suggesting surveillance or investigation.
  • 死矣 - 'He is dead,' a concise and final statement.
  • 度量宏深 - Phrase meaning 'broad-minded and deep in character,' indicating a noble and generous disposition.
  • 建德路达噜噶齐衮都罗丹 - Full name of a Yuan official: 'Gundu Rada, the Duluqai of Jiande Road.' '达噜噶齐' (Daruqai) is a title, and '衮都罗丹' (Gundu Rada) is the name.

  • 任满听除 - 'After completing his term, he was awaiting assignment to a new post.'

  • 即日録用 - 'Was immediately appointed,' indicating swift action.
  • 持俸钞五十定餽之 - 'Carried fifty dinars of salary to present to him,' where '俸钞' is salary in paper currency and '五十定' is fifty units of currency.
  • 家甚贫 - 'His family was very poor,' indicating financial hardship.
  • 朋友间每分财以济 - 'Among his friends, he often shared wealth to help others,' indicating generosity.
  • 匿其钞 - 'Hid the money,' indicating dishonesty or theft.
  • 数得书而谢不及 - 'Received many letters and was unable to thank him,' indicating repeated attempts at gratitude.
  • 疑焉 - 'He became suspicious,' indicating doubt or suspicion.
  • 具书请失谢之罪 - 'Wrote a letter to request forgiveness for the mistake,' indicating a formal apology.

  • 为其恶 - 'Spoke of his evil,' with '为' meaning 'to speak of' or 'to tell about,' and '其恶' meaning 'his evil.'

  • 提控 - A term for a lower-level official responsible for oversight or control, often in administrative or judicial contexts.
  • 掾 - A clerk or subordinate official in a government office.
  • 达噜噶齐 - A title used in the Yuan dynasty for officials of high rank, often translated as 'Daruqai' or 'Dulugaqi.'
  • 建德路 - A Yuan dynasty administrative region, likely corresponding to modern-day Jian County in Zhejiang Province.
  • 贑州路 - Another administrative region, likely corresponding to modern-day Ganzhou in Jiangxi Province.
  • 穆巴喇 - A name of a Yuan dynasty official, possibly of Turkic or Mongol origin, and likely a governor or administrator.
  • 俸钞 - Salary paid in paper currency, common in the Yuan dynasty.
  • 五十定 - A unit of currency; the exact value is uncertain, but it refers to a specific amount of money.
  • 西域人 - Refers to people from the Western Regions, which in this context likely includes Central Asia or regions west of China.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 寒号虫 - A specific type of insect, known for its unusual behavior and name, which translates to 'cold-calling insect' or 'cold-calling creature.' The name itself is an idiom, reflecting the insect's peculiar habit of calling out in cold weather.
  • 四足有肉翅不能飞 - Literally means 'four-footed with fleshy wings unable to fly,' describing the physical characteristics of the insect.
  • 其粪即五灵脂 - The insect's droppings are used as a medicinal substance, known as 'five-spirit fat' or 'five-spirit resin,' which is a traditional Chinese medicine.
  • 当盛暑时文采绚烂 - During the hot summer, the insect's appearance is bright and colorful, suggesting a period of beauty and vitality.
  • 比至深冬严寒之际毛羽脱落索然如鷇雏 - By the time it reaches the severe winter, its feathers fall off, leaving it looking like a newborn bird, indicating a period of decline and vulnerability.
  • 遂自鸣曰得过且过 - It then calls out, 'Just get by,' which reflects a state of resignation and survival.
  • 嗟夫世之人中无所守者率不甘湛涪乡里必振拔自豪 - Alas, people who have nothing to hold onto generally do not content themselves with their current status but seek to rise and be proud.
  • 求尺寸名诧九族侪则便志满意得出肆入以为天下无复我加矣 - They seek a small name to impress their relatives and peers, feeling satisfied and believing they are unmatched in the world.
  • 及乎稍遇贬抑遽若防家之狗垂首贴耳揺尾乞怜惟恐人不我恤视寒号虫何异哉 - When they encounter slight criticism, they immediately become like a dog in a kennel, hanging their heads, pressing their ears, and wagging their tails to beg for mercy, fearing no one will care for them, which is no different from the cold-calling insect.
  • 是可哀已 - This is indeed pitiful.
  • 尝见人戱呼一哗讦者为邓思贤 - Once, someone jokingly called a noisy and slanderous person 'Deng Sixian.'
  • 初不可晓后读笔谈始得其说 - At first, it was not understood, but after reading *The Brush Talk* (a historical text), the meaning became clear.
  • 世江西人好讼 - It is said that people from Jiangxi province are fond of lawsuits.
  • 有一书名邓思贤皆讼牒法也 - There is a book named *Deng Sixian* that contains legal documents and methods for lawsuits.
  • 其始则教以侮文侮不可得则欺诬以取之欺诬不可得则求其罪刦之 - Initially, it teaches to insult and defame; if that does not work, it resorts to deceit and slander; if that fails, it seeks to incriminate and coerce.
  • 盖思贤人名也人其术遂以名书村校中往往以授生徒 - Deng Sixian is a person's name, and because of his methods, the book is named after him. Village schools often teach these methods to students.

  • 蜂窠 - A bee hive, often used metaphorically to describe intricate or complex structures.

  • 良姜 - Galangal, a medicinal herb used in traditional Chinese medicine.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 世系 - Genealogy or lineage; used in classical Chinese to denote family ancestry and lineage records.
  • 古人重谱系 - Ancient people valued genealogies; emphasizes the cultural importance of recording family lineages in antiquity.
  • 渊明命子诗云 - Yuanming composed a poem for his son; refers to a poem by Tao Yuanming (陶渊明), a famous poet of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
  • 爰及苖裔 - Extending to descendants; refers to the continuation of the oath through generations.
  • 所谓防誓山河谓此盟也 - This is what is meant by 'swearing by the mountains and rivers'—this pact.
  • 此盖谓青也 - This refers to Qing (青); a name or title of a figure in the lineage.
  • 语黙隆窊以言自青后未有显者也 - The silence and decline from Qing onward indicate that no one after Qing became prominent.
  • 渊明乃长沙公之曾孙 - Yuanming was the great-grandson of the Marquis of Changsha; identifying his lineage.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 宋马永卿 - Ma Yongqing (马永卿) of the Song dynasty, a scholar and compiler of classical texts.
  • 渊明 - Tao Yuanming (陶渊明), a famous poet of the Eastern Jin dynasty, known for his reclusive lifestyle and poetic works.
  • 愍侯 - Marquis of Mourning, a title given to a noble who had significant military or political contributions.
  • 汉功臣表 - Han Gongchen Biao (汉功臣表), the official record of Han Dynasty meritorious ministers, likely from Records of the Grand Historian (Shiji) by Sima Qian.
  • 左司马 - Left司马, a military rank or title in the Han dynasty.
  • 开封 - Kaifeng, an ancient city in Henan province, historically an important capital.
  • 代 - The state of Dai, a region conquered by the Han dynasty.
  • 黄河 - The Yellow River, a major river in China.
  • 泰山 - Mount Tai, a sacred mountain in Shandong.
  • 长沙公 - The Marquis of Changsha, a title held by an ancestor of Tao Yuanming.
  • 侃 - Tao Kan (陶侃), a military general and statesman of the Eastern Jin dynasty, and an ancestor of Tao Yuanming.
  • 谱籍 - Genealogical records or family registers.
  • 士大夫 - Gentry or scholar-officials, the elite class in traditional Chinese society.

Proper Nouns

  • 宋马永卿 - Ma Yongqing (马永卿), a scholar of the Song dynasty.
  • 渊明 - Tao Yuanming (陶渊明), a famous poet of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
  • 愍侯 - Marquis of Mourning (愍侯), a title given to a noble.
  • 汉功臣表 - Han Gongchen Biao (汉功臣表), the official record of Han Dynasty meritorious ministers.

[360]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 辍耕録 - The title of the text, meaning 'Records of a Tilled Field,' referring to the author's personal notes or observations.
  • 临摹硬黄响榻 - A set of terms referring to different techniques in copying or reproducing ancient texts or artworks. '临摹' means 'to copy by sight,' '硬黄' refers to a type of yellow paper used for copying, '响榻' involves tracing over a text by holding a light source behind the original.
  • 欵识 - Refers to inscriptions or marks on ancient objects, often used to authenticate them.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 宋番昜张世南宦防纪闻 - Refers to a historical record written by Zhang Shinan, a scholar from the Song Dynasty, detailing his experiences and observations.
  • 辨博书画古器 - The practice of studying and authenticating ancient paintings, calligraphy, and artifacts, which was highly valued in Chinese culture.
  • 临渊之临 - A metaphorical expression meaning 'to observe from a distance,' often used in the context of careful observation and study.
  • 腊茶色朱砂斑真青緑井口之 - Describes the authentic appearance of ancient objects, emphasizing their historical and artistic value.
  • 云纹雷纹山纹轻重雷纹垂花雷纹鳞纹细纹粟纹蝉纹黄目飞廉饕餮蛟螭虬龙麟鳯熊虎蛇鹿马象鸾防牺蜼鳬双鱼蟠虺如意圜络 - These patterns are deeply rooted in Chinese mythology and symbolism, each representing different aspects of nature, power, and virtue.

Proper Nouns

  • 宋番昜张世南 - The author of the historical record, Zhang Shinan, who lived during the Song Dynasty.
  • 钦定四库全书 - The 'Four Treasuries,' a massive imperial collection of Chinese literature compiled during the Qing Dynasty.
  • 云纹雷纹山纹轻重雷纹垂花雷纹鳞纹细纹粟纹蝉纹黄目飞廉饕餮蛟螭虬龙麟鳯熊虎蛇鹿马象鸾防牺蜼鳬双鱼蟠虺如意圜络 - These are specific patterns and motifs found on ancient bronze objects, each with its own name and significance.

[361]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 盘云百乳鹦耳贯耳偃耳直耳附耳挟耳兽耳虎耳兽足防足百兽三螭穟草瑞草带【若蚪结之势】星带【四旁饰以星象】辅乳【钟名用以节乐者】碎乳【钟名大乳三十六外复有小乳周之】立防双防之凡古器制度一有合此则以名之如云雷钟鹿马洗鹦耳壶之是也 - This is a highly descriptive and compound phrase that lists various types of ancient artifacts and their features, such as 'cloud and thunder bell,' 'deer and horse basin,' 'parrot-ear pot,' etc. Each term is a specific type of artifact with distinct characteristics.
  • 如有欵识则以欵识名如周叔液鼎齐侯钟之是也 - This phrase means that if an artifact has inscriptions, it is named according to those inscriptions, as seen in examples like the 'Zhou Shuyi Ding' and 'Qi Hou Zhong.'
  • 古器之名则有钟【大曰特中曰镈小曰编】鼎尊罍彛舟【洗而有耳】卣【音酉又音由中尊器也有攀盖足壶】瓶爵斗【有耳有流有足流即觜也】巵觯【之豉切酒觞也】角【彛而无柱】桮敦簠【其形方】簋【鼎而矮盖有四 - This section lists various types of ancient artifacts and their specific forms, such as 'bell' (钟), 'ding' (鼎), 'lei' (罍), 'yi' (彝), 'zhou' (舟), 'you' (卣), 'ping' (瓶), 'jue' (爵), 'dou' (斗), 'zhi' (觯), 'jiao' (角), 'duan' (敦), 'fu' (簠), and 'gui' (簋). Each term is a specific type of artifact with distinct features.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 周叔液鼎齐侯钟 - These are specific examples of ancient artifacts with inscriptions, named after their historical contexts. 'Zhou Shuyi Ding' and 'Qi Hou Zhong' are well-known artifacts from ancient Chinese history.
  • 云雷钟鹿马洗鹦耳壶 - These are specific types of ancient artifacts, each with unique designs and uses. 'Cloud and thunder bell,' 'deer and horse basin,' and 'parrot-ear pot' are examples of artifacts that reflect the cultural and artistic styles of their time.

Proper Nouns

  • 周叔液鼎 - The 'Zhou Shuyi Ding,' a specific type of ancient artifact with inscriptions.
  • 齐侯钟 - The 'Qi Hou Zhong,' another specific type of ancient artifact with inscriptions.
  • 云雷钟 - The 'Cloud and Thunder Bell,' a specific type of ancient artifact.
  • 鹿马洗 - The 'Deer and Horse Basin,' a specific type of ancient artifact.
  • 鹦耳壶 - The 'Parrot-Ear Pot,' a specific type of ancient artifact.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 牛偃切无底甑 - Suggests a method of cutting or shaping, while '无底甑' refers to a bottomless steamer or cooking vessel.
  • 锭 - Can mean a lump or ingot, and the multiple readings suggest different contexts or uses.
  • 斚觚鬲 - Types of ritual vessels, with '鬲' specifically referring to a vessel with hollow legs.
  • 鍑 - A large cooking vessel similar to a pot but with a ring handle.
  • 盉 - A ritual vessel with a lid, spout, and handle, used for mixing flavors.
  • 壶 - Refers to different types of pots or jars, including round, flat, square, and warm.
  • 盫 - A large pot or basin with a wide waist, legs, and handles.
  • 瓿 - A short pot or jar.
  • 铺 - Refers to a tray or dish used for spreading out offerings.
  • 罂 - A large jar or pot.
  • 鉴 - A water container with a perforated bottom, often placed on a tray.
  • 匜 - A ritual vessel used for washing hands or face.
  • 盘洗盆鋗 - Small basins or washing containers.
  • 杅磬錞铎征 - Types of musical instruments, likely smaller versions of bells.
  • 铙戚镦 - Decorative handles for objects.
  • 奁鉴 - Refers to a mirror.
  • 节钺戈矛盾弩机表坐旂铃刀 - Various military and ceremonial items, including banners, flags, and weapons.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 牛偃切无底甑 - Steaming and cooking methods were important in ancient Chinese cuisine.
  • 锭 - The term '锭' could refer to precious metals or other materials used in rituals or daily life.
  • 斚觚鬲 - Ritual vessels like '斚觚鬲' were used in ancestral worship and other ceremonies.
  • 鍑 - Large cooking vessels were essential in ancient Chinese households.
  • 盉 - Mixing vessels were used in preparing and serving food.
  • 壶 - Different types of pots and jars were used for storing and serving various items.
  • 盫 - Large basins were used for washing and other household tasks.
  • 瓿 - Short pots and jars were used for various purposes.
  • 铺 - Trays and dishes were used for spreading out offerings during rituals.
  • 罂 - Large jars and pots were used for storage and cooking.
  • 鉴 - Water containers were used for personal hygiene and other purposes.
  • 匜 - Washing vessels were essential for personal cleanliness.
  • 盘洗盆鋗 - Small basins were used for washing hands and face.
  • 杅磬錞铎征 - Musical instruments were used in ceremonies and performances.
  • 铙戚镦 - Decorative handles were used on various objects.
  • 奁鉴 - Mirrors were used for personal grooming and decoration.
  • 节钺戈矛盾弩机表坐旂铃刀 - Military and ceremonial items were used in battles and rituals.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The long list of items and their descriptions must be read as a single catalogue of ritual and practical objects.

[363]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 筆杖頭蹲龍 - A decorative element on a writing implement or staff, symbolizing power and authority.
  • 宮廟乗舆之饰 - Decorations on imperial carriages and palace items, often featuring dragons and other symbols of imperial power.
  • 鸠车 - A traditional toy for children, often a small carriage or vehicle.
  • 车辂托辕之属 - Components of a carriage, particularly those that support the axles, often used in ceremonial contexts.
  • 欵识 - Inscriptions or marks on objects, with '欵' referring to the recessed part and '识' to the protruding part.
  • 腊茶色 - The color of tea that has been dried or preserved, often a sign of age and quality.
  • 三代及秦汉间器 - Artifacts from the Three Dynasties (Xia, Shang, Zhou) and the Qin and Han periods, significant in Chinese history and culture.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 双鹰名冠古今 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'The double eagles are renowned throughout history.'
  • 愿托君为重觉而怪之未卜其何祥 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'Wishing to entrust you with great importance, I feel strange and cannot predict what omen this is.'
  • 迟明欲行忽主人携鹰图来且四轴余意丽人双鹰符此数耳 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'Just before dawn, I was about to leave when the host brought four scrolls of eagle paintings. My thoughts were filled with the beauty of the double eagles, which seemed to match this number.'
  • 继出一小轴乃梦所见有诗四十字跋语九十八识曰泰和丁卯出蒲东普救僧舍绘唐崔氏莺莺真十洲种玉大志宜之题画诗书皆絶神品也余惊诧良乆时有司羣官吏环视因缩不目托以跋语佳胜赎之吁物理相感果何如耶岂法书名画自有灵耶抑名不朽者随神耶遇合有定数耶 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'Then a smaller scroll was presented, which depicted what I had seen in a dream. It contained forty characters of poetry and ninety-eight words of colophon, identifying it as from the year Dingmao in the Taihe era, created in the temple of Puxi in Pu East, depicting the true beauty of Tang Dynasty's Cui Yanying. The painting, poetry, and calligraphy are all divine masterpieces. I was amazed for a long time. Officials and officials surrounded me, and I felt embarrassed. I entrusted the colophon, which was praised for its excellence, and redeemed it. Alas, how do physical and spiritual matters resonate? Do famous calligraphy and paintings have their own spirit? Or do immortal names follow the spirit? Is there a predetermined fate in their meeting?'
  • 余尝谓闗雎硕人姿徳兼备君子之配也琴心雪句才艶聨芳文士之偶也自诗书道废丈夫弗学况女流乎故近世非无秀色往往脂粉腥鸦鳯莫辨求其彷佛待月章之万一絶代无闻焉此亦 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'I have often said that the *Guanju* and the *Shuo Ren* combine beauty and virtue, making them suitable for a gentleman. The *Qin Xin* and *Xue Ju* combine talent and beauty, making them suitable for a scholar. Since the decline of the *Shi* and *Shu* traditions, men no longer learn, let alone women. Therefore, in recent times, there are no outstanding beauties. Often, the beauty is tainted by the mundane, and the distinction between noble and common is blurred. To find a resemblance to the moon's light is rare. This is also a lament.'

  • 因归于我义弗辞已 - This phrase means 'I accept it as my duty and cannot refuse it.' It expresses a sense of obligation or responsibility.

  • 盖前金赵愚轩之字 - This phrase means 'the former Jin Zhao Yuxuan's name.' It introduces a historical figure.
  • 曾为巩西簿 - This phrase means 'once served as the western registrar of Gong.' It describes a historical role.
  • 遗山谓泰和有诗名 - This phrase means 'Yi Shan said that Taihe had a reputation for poetry.' It introduces a historical reference.
  • 五言平淡 - This phrase means 'plain and simple five-character verse.' It describes a style of poetry.

  • 然二君之风韵可想见矣 - This phrase means 'yet the style of the two gentlemen can be imagined.' It suggests that their style can be inferred.

  • 就为録文扵上 - This phrase means 'he then copied the text onto it.' It describes an action.

  • 因丁文雅军扰掠蒲人 - This phrase means 'because of the disturbances and pillaging by Ding Wenya's army in Pu.' It describes a historical event.

  • 请吏防之不及扵难 - This phrase means 'he asked the officials to protect her, but it was too late.' It describes an action and its outcome.

  • 姨之弱子幼女当以仁兄之礼奉承 - This phrase means 'your young son and daughter should be treated as if they were my nephews and nieces.' It describes a request.
  • 命莺莺出 - This phrase means 'he ordered Yingying to come out.' It describes an action.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 拜顔色艶异光辉动人生 - A poetic description of someone's appearance that is striking and captivating, literally 'the color of the face is gorgeous and radiant, moving people.'
  • 问其年纪 - Literally 'ask about his/her age,' a common phrase used to inquire about someone's age.
  • 私礼莺莺之侍婢红娘间道其意 - A complex phrase indicating that the person secretly communicated with Yingying's maid Hongniang to convey their intentions.
  • 诗章往复遂酬所愿 - Literally 'through the exchange of poems, they fulfilled their wishes,' indicating a mutual understanding and agreement.
  • 中间离合多故然不能终谐伉俪 - Literally 'in the middle of their relationship, there were many reasons for separation, so they could not ultimately become a harmonious couple,' describing the ups and downs of their relationship.
  • 说者以为生即张子野宋王性之着传竒辨正 - Literally 'those who say that the protagonist is Zhang Ziyi, Song Wang Xingzhi, and wrote the strange biography to correct it,' indicating a scholarly debate about the identity of the protagonist.
  • 按防之作姨母郑氏墓铭云其既防夫遭军乱防之为保护其家 - Literally 'according to the defense work, the tombstone of Aunt Zheng said that after her husband was killed in the military uprising, she protected her family,' describing the actions of Aunt Zheng.
  • 又作陆氏志云余外祖睦州刺史郑济白乐天作防之母郑氏志亦言郑济女 - Literally 'and the *Lu* clan's record said that my maternal grandfather, the governor of Muzhou, Zheng Ji, and Bai Juyi wrote the tombstone of Fang's mother Zheng, also saying that Zheng Ji's daughter,' indicating multiple sources confirming the identity of Zheng Ji's daughter.
  • 唐崔氏谱永宁尉鹏娶郑济女则莺莺乃崔鹏之女扵防之为中表也 - Literally 'the Tang *Cui* clan's genealogy says that Yongning County Magistrate Peng married Zheng Ji's daughter, so Yingying is Peng's daughter and a cousin to Fang,' clarifying the family relationships.
  • 乐天作防之墓志以太和五年薨年五十三即当以大歴十四年己未生至贞元庚辰正二十二嵗 - Literally 'Bai Juyi wrote the tombstone of Fang, saying that he died in the fifth year of Taihe, aged fifty-three, so he was born in the fourteenth year of Dali, the year of Guiwei, and was twenty-two in the first year of Zhenguan,' providing a timeline for Fang's life.
  • 凡此数端决为防之无疑特托他姓以避就耳 - Literally 'all these points decisively confirm that it is Fang without doubt, but he is using another surname to avoid or achieve something,' indicating a scholarly consensus on the identity of the protagonist.
  • 事具侯鲭録中 - Literally 'the details are recorded in *Hou Qinglu*,' referring to a historical record.

  • 刻者运笔中疑心手相戾勾画之际妄生圭角也 - Means 'the flaw of 'ke' (stiffness) occurs when the mind and hand are at odds, leading to sharp and unnatural angles.'

  • 各有辨长幅横卷裂纹横横幅直卷裂纹直各随轴势裂也 - Describes the direction of cracks in different types of scrolls: long scrolls with horizontal cracks and horizontal scrolls with vertical cracks, all following the direction of the scroll's axis.
  • 直防不当一缕歳乆卷自两头苏开防不相合 - Describes the condition of a scroll over time: if a crack is not aligned with the threads, after many years, the scroll will naturally split from both ends, and the parts will not fit together.
  • 不作毛搯亦苏不可僞作 - Describes a crack that will not form a frayed edge and cannot be artificially created.
  • 其僞者快刀直过当缕两头依旧生作毛起搯又坚纫也 - Describes the characteristics of a fake crack: the fake one will be cut straight through the threads, with both ends still frayed, and it will be strong and durable.
  • 湿染者色栖缕间干熏者烟臭上深下浅 - Describes the appearance of dyed and smoked silk: those dyed in wet are colored between the threads, and those smoked have a smoky odor, with the color deeper at the top and lighter at the bottom.
  • 古素有一般古香真绢色淡虽百破而色明白精神采色如新 - Describes the characteristics of ancient silk: ancient silk has a unique ancient fragrance, the color of true silk is light, and even if broken into a hundred pieces, the color remains bright and the colors are vivid and fresh.
  • 惟佛像多经香烟熏损本色染绢作温香色栖尘文间最易辨 - Describes the characteristics of silk used for Buddhist images: Buddhist images are often damaged by incense smoke, the original color is altered, and the silk is dyed with a warm fragrance, making it easy to distinguish.
  • 仍盖色上作一重古破不直裂湏连两三经不可僞作 - Describes the characteristics of ancient silk: the color is layered, and the cracks are not straight but connected through two or three threads, making it impossible to fake.
  • 国朝东楚汤垕字君载号采真子着画鉴一卷论歴代名画悉有依据其杂论曰 - Introduces the author and his work: During the reign of the Eastern Han Dynasty in the south, Tang Hou, also known as Junzai and Cai Zhenzi, wrote a volume titled *Painting Critique* that discusses famous paintings from all dynasties, all based on evidence. His miscellaneous commentary says...
  • 古人作画皆有深意运思落笔莫不各有所主况名下无虚士相传既乆必有过人处 - Describes the nature of ancient painting: ancient painters all had deep intentions, and their thoughts and strokes were always guided by some purpose. Famous painters are never empty names, and after being passed down for a long time, they must have exceptional qualities.
  • 今人看画出自己见不经师授不阅记録但合其意者为 - Describes the modern approach to art: modern people judge paintings based on their own opinions, without formal instruction or reading records, and only those that match their preferences are considered good.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 布绢素百破必好画裂文 - Refers to the traditional Chinese practice of using broken silk for painting cracks, which is a specific cultural practice.
  • 古素有一般古香真绢色淡虽百破而色明白精神采色如新 - Refers to the unique characteristics of ancient silk, which is a material with historical and cultural significance in Chinese art.
  • 惟佛像多经香烟熏损本色染绢作温香色栖尘文间最易辨 - Refers to the use of silk for Buddhist images, which is a significant cultural and religious practice in Chinese history.
  • 国朝东楚汤垕字君载号采真子着画鉴一卷论歴代名画悉有依据其杂论曰 - Refers to the author and his work, *Painting Critique*, which is a historical and cultural text on Chinese painting.
  • 古人作画皆有深意运思落笔莫不各有所主况名下无虚士相传既乆必有过人处 - Refers to the traditional Chinese approach to painting, which emphasizes depth of thought and intention, a cultural value in Chinese art.

Proper Nouns

  • 国朝东楚汤垕字君载号采真子 - The name of the author, Tang Hou, also known as Junzai and Cai Zhenzi.
  • 画鉴 - The title of the author's work, *Painting Critique*.

[394]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 佳不合其意者为不佳 - Compound phrase meaning 'if a painting does not meet one's taste, it is considered bad.' Reflects a subjective standard of aesthetic judgment.
  • 问其如何是佳则茫然失对 - Compound phrase meaning 'when asked what makes a painting good, one is at a loss for words.' Highlights the difficulty in articulating aesthetic criteria.
  • 迂濶之意见图画爱玩不去手 - Compound phrase meaning 'having a broad and leisurely interest in paintings, one cannot put them down.' Describes a deep and continuous engagement with art.
  • 鉴赏之士便加礼问 - Compound phrase meaning 'one respects and asks those who appreciate art.' Emphasizes the importance of seeking guidance from knowledgeable individuals.
  • 始有少悟 - Compound phrase meaning 'one begins to have a slight understanding.' Indicates the gradual acquisition of knowledge.
  • 灯下不可看画 - Compound phrase meaning 'one should not look at paintings under dim light.' Advises against viewing art in poor lighting conditions.
  • 醉余酒边不可看画 - Compound phrase meaning 'one should not look at paintings after drinking.' Warns against viewing art in a state of intoxication.
  • 俗客尤不可示之 - Compound phrase meaning 'especially not to show it to vulgar guests.' Emphasizes the importance of showing art to the right audience.
  • 卷舒不得其法最为害物 - Compound phrase meaning 'not handling scrolls properly is the most harmful.' Highlights the importance of proper care in handling art.
  • 庸人孺子见画必看妄加雌黄品藻 - Compound phrase meaning 'ordinary people and children will look at paintings and rashly comment on them.' Criticizes the tendency of untrained viewers to make hasty judgments.
  • 本不识物乱订真伪 - Compound phrase meaning 'they do not know the subject and rashly determine its authenticity.' Further criticizes the lack of expertise among untrained viewers.
  • 令人短气 - Compound phrase meaning 'it is disheartening.' Expresses the frustration caused by such behavior.
  • 古人画藁谓之粉本 - Compound phrase meaning 'ancient artists' sketches were called 'powder models.' Refers to preliminary drawings used as templates.
  • 前辈多寳畜之 - Compound phrase meaning 'predecessors often treasured them.' Indicates the high value placed on these sketches.
  • 盖其草草不经意处有天然之妙 - Compound phrase meaning 'because even the carelessly drawn parts have a natural charm.' Highlights the unexpected beauty in spontaneous sketches.
  • 宣和绍兴所藏粉本多有神妙 - Compound phrase meaning 'the powder models collected during the Xuanhe and Shaoxing periods are often marvelously crafted.' Refers to specific dynastic periods known for their artistic achievements.
  • 古人作画有得意者多再作之 - Compound phrase meaning 'ancient artists often repeated their best works.' Describes the practice of revisiting and refining successful compositions.
  • 如李成寒林范寛雪山王诜烟江叠嶂不可枚举 - Compound phrase meaning 'like Li Cheng's 'Cold Forest,' Fan Kuan's 'Snowy Mountains,' and Wang Shen's 'Foggy River with Overlapping Peaks,' there are countless examples.' Lists notable artists and their works.
  • 看画如看美人其风神骨相有体肌之外者 - Compound phrase meaning 'looking at paintings is like looking at beautiful women; their spirit and form go beyond physical appearance.' Uses a metaphor to describe the deeper qualities of art.
  • 今人看古迹必先求形似次及傅染次及事实 - Compound phrase meaning 'modern people looking at ancient works first seek resemblance, then color application, and finally factual accuracy.' Outlines a hierarchical approach to appreciating art.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 殊非赏鉴之法也 - This phrase means 'this is not the way of appreciation,' indicating that the method described is incorrect or inadequate.
  • 好事家与赏鉴家自是两等 - This phrase distinguishes between 'collectors' (好事家) and 'appreciators' (赏鉴家), suggesting that they are two different types of people.
  • 多资力贪好名胜遇物收置不过聴声 - This phrase describes collectors as those who have resources and are greedy for famous and valuable items, collecting them without much thought or understanding.
  • 若鉴赏则天资高明多阅传録或自能画或深画意毎得一图终日寳玩如对古人不能夺也 - This phrase describes appreciators as having high natural talent, extensive knowledge, and deep understanding of painting. They treasure each piece they acquire and treat it as if it were created by an ancient master.
  • 观六朝画先观绢素次观笔法次观气韵大槩十中可信者一二 - This phrase outlines the process of appreciating Six Dynasties paintings, starting with the quality of the material, then the brushwork, and finally the spirit and style. It suggests that out of ten such paintings, only one or two can be fully trusted.
  • 有御府题印者尤不可信 - This phrase indicates that paintings with official seals from the imperial treasury are particularly unreliable.
  • 古画东移西掇挦补成章此弊自高宗朝庄宗古始也 - This phrase describes a common practice in ancient paintings where parts were moved around and patched together, a problem that started during the reign of Emperor Gaozong and continued into the Song dynasty.
  • 余友人吴兴夏文彦字士良号兰渚生其家世藏名迹鲜有比者朝夕玩索心领神防加以游于画艺悟入厥趣是故鉴赏品藻万不失一 - This phrase describes the author's friend, Xia Wenyan, who is a collector and appreciator of fine art. His family has a rich collection of famous works, and he studies them daily, gaining a deep understanding and appreciation of their artistic value.
  • 因取各画记图画见闻志画继续画记为本叅以宣和画谱南渡七朝画史齐梁魏陈唐宋以来诸家画録及传记杂说百氏之书捜潜剔秘网罗无遗自轩辕时至宋幼主徳祐乙 - This phrase explains the sources used by the author to compile his work, including various records, catalogs, and histories of painting from different dynasties, as well as other literary works.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 六朝 - Refers to the Six Dynasties period (220–589 AD), a time of significant cultural and artistic development in China.
  • 御府 - Refers to the imperial treasury, which often authenticated and stored valuable artworks.
  • 高宗朝 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Gaozong of Song (1127–1162 AD), a period known for its cultural and artistic achievements.
  • 吴兴 - Refers to Wuxing, a region in present-day Zhejiang Province, known for its cultural and artistic heritage.
  • 宣和画谱 - Refers to the *Xuanhe Hua Pu* (宣和画谱), a catalog of paintings compiled during the Song dynasty.
  • 南渡七朝 - Refers to the Southern Song dynasty, which ruled after the fall of the Northern Song in 1127.
  • 齐梁魏陈唐宋 - Refers to the dynasties of Qi, Liang, Wei, Chen, Tang, and Song, covering a significant period of Chinese history.
  • 轩辕 - Refers to the legendary Yellow Emperor, a mythical figure in Chinese history.
  • 宋幼主徳祐乙 - Refers to the last emperor of the Song dynasty, Emperor Duzong (1264–1274 AD), whose reign ended with the Mongol conquest.

Proper Nouns

  • 夏文彦 - The name of the author's friend, a collector and appreciator of fine art.
  • 士良 - The courtesy name of Xia Wenyan.
  • 兰渚生 - The pseudonym of Xia Wenyan.
  • 宣和画谱 - The *Xuanhe Hua Pu*, a catalog of paintings compiled during the Song dynasty.
  • 南渡七朝 - The Southern Song dynasty.
  • 轩辕 - The legendary Yellow Emperor.
  • 宋幼主徳祐乙 - Emperor Duzong of Song.

  • 大石左曰敷锡神运万歳峰右曰玉京独秀太平岩 - Describes two large stones with inscriptions on them, one on the left and one on the right, likely symbolic or commemorative.
  • 山莊 - A mountain villa or retreat within the palace complex, a place for relaxation and contemplation.
  • 翠防閣 - A pavilion named for its green protective function, possibly a lookout or viewing platform.
  • 仙韶院 - The Music Academy, where musicians and performers were trained, a center for the arts.
  • 涌翠峰 - A peak or hill named for its green, flowing appearance, possibly a scenic spot.
  • 大涤涌翠东连长生殿殿东曰涌金殿 - Describes a series of buildings connected by their names, possibly a complex of pavilions or halls, each with its own function or significance.

[399]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 東曰蓬萊殿長生 - Directional phrase meaning 'to the east is the Penglai Hall of Longevity.' This structure is repeated throughout the text to describe the spatial layout of the palace complex.
  • 浮玉之西曰瀛洲殿 - 'To the west of Fuyu is the Yingzhou Hall.' This pattern is used to describe the location of each building or hall.
  • 閱武南曰內藏庫 - 'To the south of Yuewu is the Inner Treasure Store.' This is a standard way of indicating spatial relationships in Classical Chinese.
  • 由嚴祗門 - 'From the Yanzhi Gate.' This introduces a point of reference from which the spatial layout is described.
  • 本明俊殿試進士之所 - 'Originally the Hall of Bright Virtue, where the imperial examination for jinshi (advanced degree) was held.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 長生 - 'Longevity' or 'immortality,' a common theme in imperial architecture and titles, especially in the context of the emperor's desire for eternal rule.
  • 閱武 - 'Reviewing the military' or 'military review,' indicating a hall used for military parades or training.
  • 夀聖宮 - A palace or residence associated with longevity and blessings, often used for the empress dowager.
  • 明俊殿 - A hall used for the imperial examinations, a critical aspect of the Confucian bureaucratic system.
  • 兩宮太后 - Refers to the two empresses dowager, typically the mother and grandmother of the reigning emperor.

[400]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 军司徽音夀圣之东曰太后苑 - Descriptive phrase indicating the location of the 'Imperial Noble Lady's Garden' (太后苑) to the east of the '徽音夀圣' (likely a palace or hall).
  • 苑之殿曰庆春 - Standard formula meaning 'the hall within the garden is called Qingchun.'
  • 小东华与正东华对 - Describes the relationship between two gates: the Xiaodonghua (Small Eastern Gate) and the Zhengdonghua (Main Eastern Gate), which are opposite each other.
  • 东华门内正北尚廏局 - A location description: the Shangxu Bureau is situated directly north inside the Donghua Gate.
  • 尚廏西北曰临武殿 - Indicates the Linwu Hall is located northwest of the Shangxu Bureau.
  • 左掖门正北尚食局 - Describes the Shangshi Bureau as located directly north of the Zuo Ye Gate (Left Flank Gate).
  • 宫苑司西北曰尚醖局 - Indicates the Shangyun Bureau is located northwest of the Gongyuan Office (Royal Garden Office).
  • 汤药局侍仪司少西曰符寳局 - A complex spatial relationship: the Fubao Bureau is slightly to the west of the Shiyi Office (Attendant Office), which is adjacent to the Tangyao Bureau (Medicine Bureau).
  • 器物局西则撒合门 - Describes the Sahe Gate as located to the west of the Qiwu Bureau (Imperial Property Office).
  • 嘉瑞楼西曰三庙 - Indicates the Sanmiao (Three Temples) are located to the west of the Jiarui Tower (Tower of Auspicious Signs).
  • 正殿曰徳昌 - Standard formula: the main hall is called Dechang.
  • 并南向 - Indicates that all these halls face south.
  • 宫西门曰西华与东华直 - Describes the Xihua Gate (Western Gate) as being opposite the Donghua Gate (Eastern Gate).
  • 其北门曰安贞 - Indicates the Anzhen Gate is located to the north of the Xihua Gate.
  • 二大石外凡花石台榭池亭之细并不録 - A clause indicating that all minor details such as flower stones, pavilions, terraces, pools, and pavilions outside the two large stones are not recorded.
  • 观其制度简素比土阶茅茨则过矣 - A comparative phrase meaning 'observing its architectural style, it is simple and plain, surpassing even the earthen steps and thatched huts.'
  • 视汉之所谓千门万户珠璧华丽之室则无有也 - A comparison: 'compared to the Han dynasty's so-called palaces with a thousand doors and ten thousand windows adorned with pearls and jade, it has none of that.'
  • 然后之人因其制度而损益之以求其称斯可矣 - A conclusion: 'those who came after should modify and improve upon this system in order to achieve balance and appropriateness; this would be sufficient.'
  • 公又有録汴梁宫人语五言絶句一十九首 - Indicates that the author has also recorded nineteen five-character absolute verse poems composed by palace women in Bianliang (modern Kaifeng).
  • 虽一时之所寄兴亦不无有伤感之意 - A phrase meaning 'though these were composed as expressions of the moment, they also contain some sorrowful sentiment.'
  • 今并附 - A closing phrase meaning 'these are now appended [to the text].'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 军司徽音夀圣 - Likely refers to a palace or hall within the imperial complex. The name '徽音夀圣' may be a title or honorific for a palace or building, possibly related to the empress or太后 (empress dowager).
  • 太后苑 - 'Imperial Noble Lady's Garden,' a term used to describe a garden within the imperial palace complex reserved for the empress dowager.
  • 尚廏局 - The Shangxu Bureau, responsible for managing the imperial stables and horses.
  • 临武殿 - A hall or palace, the name possibly symbolic of martial strength or vigilance.
  • 左掖门 - The Zuo Ye Gate (Left Flank Gate), another gate within the palace complex.
  • 尚食局 - The Shangshi Bureau, responsible for the imperial kitchen and food services.
  • 宫苑司 - The Gongyuan Office, responsible for the maintenance and management of the imperial gardens.
  • 尚醖局 - The Shangyun Bureau, responsible for brewing and storing wine.
  • 汤药局 - The Tangyao Bureau, responsible for preparing medicines and tonics.
  • 侍仪司 - The Shiyi Office, responsible for ceremonial and protocol duties.
  • 符寳局 - The Fubao Bureau, responsible for managing imperial seals and treasures.
  • 器物局 - The Qiwu Bureau, responsible for managing imperial furnishings and objects.
  • 撒合门 - Sahe Gate, possibly a lesser-known gate or entrance.
  • 嘉瑞楼 - Jiarui Tower, a building associated with auspicious signs or good fortune.
  • 三庙 - The Sanmiao (Three Temples), likely referring to the ancestral temples of the imperial family.
  • 宣宗庙 - The Xuanzong Temple, a temple dedicated to Emperor Xuanzong of the Tang dynasty, or possibly a later emperor with the same title.
  • 二大石 - 'Two large stones,' possibly referring to two notable stones within the palace complex or as symbolic markers.
  • 花石台榭池亭 - A descriptive phrase for ornamental features such as flower stones, pavilions, terraces, pools, and pavilions.
  • 制度简素 - 'Simple and plain architecture,' contrasting with the opulence of the Han dynasty.
  • 土阶茅茨 - 'Earthen steps and thatched huts,' a reference to the simplicity of early Chinese architecture or the ideal of frugality.
  • 千门万户珠璧华丽之室 - A reference to the grandeur of Han dynasty palaces, as described in historical texts like the Book of Han.
  • 汴梁 - Bianliang, the capital of the Northern Song dynasty (modern-day Kaifeng), known for its imperial palace and cultural significance.
  • 宫人语五言绝句 - 'Poems in five-character absolute verse composed by palace women,' a genre of poetry that was popular among the court ladies of the Song dynasty.
